mini_headings_covered: Lattice Energies from the Born-Haber Cycle

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: According to the VSEPR model, why does sulfur dioxide (\(\mathrm{SO_2}\)) have a bent shape rather than a linear shape?

options.A: Sulfur has two lone pairs that repel each other.
options.B: Sulfur has one lone pair that repels the bonding pairs, compressing the O-S-O angle below 180°.
options.C: The double bonds force a linear arrangement due to electron density.
options.D: Oxygen atoms repel each other because they are both electronegative.

questions.hint: Count electron groups around sulfur: two bonds and one lone pair.
questions.explanation: Sulfur in SO₂ has three electron groups (two bonding pairs, one lone pair). The electron geometry is trigonal planar, but the lone pair occupies one position, forcing the molecule into a bent shape. Lone pair repulsion reduces the O-S-O bond angle from 120° to about 119°.

source_trace.content_anchor: SO2 molecule can be viewed as consisting of three electron pairs on the central S atom. Of these, two are bonding pairs and one is a lone pair. The overall arrangement of three electron pairs is trigonal planar but the molecule has a bent shape.

explanations_tiered.definition: VSEPR theory predicts molecular geometry by minimizing electron pair repulsions around a central atom.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Three electron groups arrange trigonal planar. A lone pair occupies one site; atoms occupy the other two, resulting in a bent molecular shape.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine three balloons tied together. They spread out in a triangle. If one balloon is a 'lone pair' that doesn't show, the other two form a bent shape.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: SO₂ has three electron groups: S=O, S–O, and a lone pair on S. The groups arrange 120° apart (trigonal planar). The lone pair takes one direction, so the oxygen atoms are bent.
explanations_tiered.advanced: VSEPR treats double bonds as single electron groups. The lone pair–bonding pair repulsion is greater than bonding pair–bonding pair repulsion, slightly compressing the O–S–O angle from 120° to ~119°.
reasoning_steps: Draw Lewis structure of SO₂: S has two bonds and one lone pair.
Count electron groups = 3.
Electron geometry = trigonal planar (120°).
Lone pair occupies one vertex → molecular shape = bent.
misconceptions: Double bonds count as two groups. False; in VSEPR, a double bond is one group.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: SO₂: 3 groups (2 bonds + 1 lone pair) → trigonal planar electron → bent molecular.

glossary_entry.definition: Molecular geometry with a central atom bonded to two atoms and having one or two lone pairs.

flashcard.front: What is the molecular shape of SO₂ and why?
flashcard.back: Bent; central S has 3 electron groups (2 bonds + 1 lone pair) giving trigonal planar electron geometry, but lone pair forces bent shape.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: S has 3 electron groups (2 bonds, 1 lone pair) → trigonal planar electron geometry → bent molecular shape.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Nonlinear geometry due to lone pair(s) on central atom.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Compare SO₂ (bent) with CO₂ (linear, no lone pairs).
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Use VSEPR to predict molecular shapes
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. One lone pair on sulfur occupies a site, forcing bent geometry; electron groups are 3, trigonal planar electron geometry.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which set of elements, when bonded to hydrogen, can form hydrogen bonds with lone pairs on adjacent molecules?
questions.hint: The atom must be small and highly electronegative, with lone pairs.
questions.explanation: Hydrogen bonding occurs when hydrogen is covalently bonded to nitrogen, oxygen, or fluorine. These atoms are small, highly electronegative, and have lone pairs, creating a strong dipole that attracts a partially positive hydrogen from another molecule.

source_trace.content_anchor: hydrogen atoms bonded to highly electronegative atoms of nitrogen, oxygen, or fluorine

explanations_tiered.definition: A hydrogen bond is a strong dipole-dipole attraction between a hydrogen atom bonded to N, O, or F and a lone pair on another N, O, or F.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Only N, O, and F are both highly electronegative and small enough to allow close approach, creating a sufficiently strong dipole for hydrogen bonding.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Only three atoms—nitrogen, oxygen, and fluorine—can make 'extra strong' stickiness when they hold hydrogen. Others are too big or weak.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Hydrogen bonds require H bonded to N, O, or F. These atoms have high electronegativity and lone pairs, creating a δ+ H that attracts a lone pair on another molecule.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The criteria for hydrogen bonding: (1) H bonded to small, highly electronegative atom (N, O, F); (2) that atom has lone pairs; (3) acceptor also has lone pairs. Chlorine is too large; its dipole is weaker.
reasoning_steps: Recall hydrogen bonding definition.
Identify required atoms: must be small, electronegative, with lone pairs.
Known set: N, O, F only.
misconceptions: Hydrogen bonding occurs with any electronegative atom. False; Cl, S are too large and less electronegative.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: H-bond: H–N, H–O, or H–F only.

glossary_entry.definition: Strong dipole-dipole attraction involving H bonded to N, O, or F.

flashcard.front: Which three elements form hydrogen bonds when bonded to hydrogen?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Which atoms can participate in hydrogen bonding as the donor?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: N, O, F (when bonded to H).
publishing_metadata.revision_note: H-bond: H with N, O, or F only.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Explain why Cl doesn't work: too large, lower electronegativity.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Identify molecules that exhibit hydrogen bonding
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. N, O, F are the only atoms that form hydrogen bonds when bonded to hydrogen.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Why does the boiling point of noble gases increase from helium to radon, even though they do not form chemical bonds?

options.A: Covalent bond strength increases down the group.
options.B: London dispersion forces increase with atomic mass and number of electrons.
options.C: Ionic interactions become stronger as atoms get larger.
options.D: Hydrogen bonding occurs in heavier noble gases.

questions.hint: Consider what changes as atomic number increases: electron cloud size and polarizability.
questions.explanation: Noble gases are monatomic and nonpolar. The only intermolecular forces are London (dispersion) forces. As atomic mass increases, the electron cloud becomes larger and more polarizable, increasing the strength of temporary dipoles and raising boiling points.

source_trace.content_anchor: London forces tend to increase with molecular weight

explanations_tiered.definition: London dispersion forces are temporary attractive forces caused by instantaneous dipoles due to electron movement.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Larger atoms have more electrons and more polarizable electron clouds, leading to stronger temporary dipoles and greater London forces.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Bigger atoms are like bigger, fluffier pillows. They can squish and create temporary 'suck' (dipoles) more easily, making them stick together more than small atoms.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Heavier noble gases have more electrons. Their electron clouds are more easily distorted (polarizable), creating stronger instantaneous dipoles and stronger London forces, requiring more energy to separate (higher bp).
explanations_tiered.advanced: London force strength is proportional to polarizability, which scales with atomic volume and electron count. The boiling point trend He (4 K) to Rn (211 K) directly correlates with increasing London dispersion.
reasoning_steps: Identify that noble gases are nonpolar, so only London forces apply.
Recall that London forces increase with number of electrons and polarizability.
Larger atoms (more electrons) → stronger London forces → higher boiling points.
misconceptions: Noble gases bond covalently. False; they are monatomic, only intermolecular forces.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: London forces ↑ with electrons/atom size → bp ↑.

glossary_entry.definition: Weak intermolecular forces arising from instantaneous dipoles.

flashcard.front: Why does boiling point increase down Group 18 (noble gases)?
flashcard.back: London dispersion forces increase with atomic mass and electron count.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What causes increasing boiling points in noble gases?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Stronger London dispersion forces due to more electrons and greater polarizability.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: More e⁻ → stronger London → higher bp.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Relate to polarizability: larger atoms have more loosely held electrons.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. London forces increase with atomic mass and electrons, raising boiling points.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: What is a metallic bond?

options.A: Sharing of delocalized valence electrons among a lattice of positively charged metal ions.
options.B: Transfer of electrons from one metal atom to another to form ions.
options.C: Covalent bonding between metal atoms using localized electron pairs.
options.D: Electrostatic attraction between discrete metal molecules.

questions.hint: The key idea is 'sea of electrons' shared among all atoms.
questions.explanation: Metallic bonding consists of metal cations immersed in a delocalized 'sea' of valence electrons. These mobile electrons are shared among all atoms in the sample, creating a strong attraction that holds the structure together.

source_trace.content_anchor: sharing of free electrons (delocalized electrons) among a lattice of positively charged metal ions

explanations_tiered.definition: A metallic bond is the electrostatic attraction between delocalized valence electrons and a lattice of metal cations.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Valence electrons are free to move throughout the metal, not attached to any specific atom. This electron sea holds the metal ions together.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine a loaf of raisin bread: the raisins are positive metal ions, and the dough is the 'sea' of electrons that holds them together.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: In metals, valence electrons are delocalized, meaning they move freely among all atoms. The attraction between these mobile electrons and the fixed cations is the metallic bond.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Metallic bonding is described by the electron-sea model (or band theory). The delocalized electrons occupy molecular orbitals that extend over the entire crystal, minimizing energy.
reasoning_steps: Recall that metals have valence electrons that are not tightly held.
Understand that these electrons are shared among many atoms (delocalized).
Resulting attraction = metallic bond.
misconceptions: Metallic bonds are like covalent bonds. False; covalent bonds localize electrons between two atoms.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Metallic bond: cations + delocalized e⁻ sea.

glossary_entry.definition: Bond formed by delocalized electrons shared among metal cations.

flashcard.front: What holds atoms together in a piece of copper metal?
flashcard.back: Metallic bonding: cations in a sea of delocalized electrons.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Attraction between delocalized valence electrons and a lattice of metal cations.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Delocalized electron sharing among metal ions.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Metallic bonding involves delocalized electrons shared among a lattice of positive ions.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: How does the electron-sea model explain the electrical conductivity of metals in the solid state?

options.A: Metal ions are free to move and carry charge.
options.B: Valence electrons are delocalized and mobile, able to flow under an applied electric field.
options.C: Covalent bonds between atoms break and reform, allowing electron flow.
options.D: Metals have a large band gap that allows electron excitation at room temperature.

questions.hint: Think about which particles are mobile in a metal.
questions.explanation: In the electron-sea model, valence electrons are not bound to specific atoms but form a mobile 'sea' around fixed cations. These delocalized electrons can move freely when an electric field is applied, creating an electric current.

source_trace.content_anchor: mobile electrons, known as conduction electrons, can transfer thermal vibration... they are good conductors of electricity

explanations_tiered.definition: The electron-sea model proposes that metal atoms pool their valence electrons into a delocalized sea surrounding positive ions.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Delocalized electrons are free to move throughout the metal, allowing them to carry charge when a voltage is applied.
explanations_tiered.beginner: In a metal, electrons are like water in a pool, free to slosh around. When you push them (electric field), they flow, creating electricity.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The electron-sea model shows valence electrons as mobile and not attached to any atom. When an electric field is applied, these conduction electrons drift, producing current.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Band theory expands on this: the valence band and conduction band overlap in metals, allowing electrons to move freely without a band gap. This explains high conductivity even at low temperatures.
reasoning_steps: Recall that in metals, valence electrons are delocalized.
Understand that delocalized electrons can move when an electric field is applied.
Therefore, metals conduct electricity in solid state.
misconceptions: Metal ions move to conduct electricity. False; only electrons move; ions are fixed in lattice.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Metals conduct: delocalized e⁻ are mobile.

glossary_entry.definition: Electrons not associated with a single atom, free to move throughout the material.

flashcard.front: Why do metals conduct electricity in the solid state?
flashcard.back: Delocalized valence electrons are mobile and can flow under an electric field.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What allows metals to conduct electricity when solid?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Delocalized (mobile) valence electrons in the electron sea.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Electrons free to move throughout metal lattice.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Contrast with ionic solids (solid = no conductivity).
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain metallic conductivity using electron-sea model
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Delocalized valence electrons are mobile and can flow, allowing conductivity.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which property of metals is NOT explained by the presence of delocalized electrons?
questions.hint: High melting point involves breaking the metallic bond, which requires energy.
questions.explanation: High melting points are due to the strength of the metallic bond (attraction between cations and delocalized electrons), which is not solely due to mobility of electrons. Malleability, conductivity, and lustre are direct results of electron delocalization.

source_trace.content_anchor: The delocalized electrons are not attached to atoms, so they can move through the metal easily. This makes metals good conductors...

explanations_tiered.definition: Malleability is the ability to be hammered into sheets; ductility is the ability to be drawn into wires.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Delocalized electrons allow atoms to slide past each other without breaking the metallic bond. This explains malleability and ductility. Conductivity and lustre also require mobile electrons.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Electrons that move freely let metal atoms shift without breaking apart, making metals bendable and shiny. But the strong hold between electrons and metal atoms (metallic bond) gives high melting points.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Malleability: layers of cations can slide because electrons are delocalized and maintain bonding. Conductivity: delocalized electrons carry charge. Lustre: mobile electrons interact with light. High melting point: strong cation-electron attraction requires energy to overcome.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Delocalization accounts for non-directional bonding, allowing slip planes (malleability). High melting point is a function of bond enthalpy (number of delocalized electrons and charge density), not just mobility.
reasoning_steps: List properties caused by delocalized electrons: conductivity, malleability, ductility, lustre.
High melting point: requires strong metallic bond, but bond strength is separate from delocalization per se.
Thus high melting point is not a direct consequence of mobility alone.
misconceptions: All metal properties are due solely to delocalized electrons. False; melting point also depends on charge and packing.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Delocalized e⁻ → conductivity, malleability, lustre. Melting point depends on bond strength too.

glossary_entry.definition: Ability of a metal to be hammered into thin sheets.

flashcard.front: Which metal property is NOT directly caused by delocalized electrons?
flashcard.back: High melting point (depends also on bond strength and charge).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What properties do delocalized electrons give metals?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Ability to be hammered into sheets.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Ask: 'What property requires breaking bonds? That's high mp.'
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Relate metal properties to delocalized electrons
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. High melting point depends on metallic bond strength, not solely on electron mobility, whereas conductivity, malleability, and lustre directly involve delocalized electrons.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: In valence bond theory, what is a sigma (\(\sigma\)) bond?

options.A: A bond formed by sideways overlap of p orbitals.
options.B: A bond formed by end-to-end overlap along the internuclear axis.
options.C: A bond formed by overlap of two d orbitals.
options.D: A bond resulting from electron transfer between atoms.

questions.hint: Sigma bonds have electron density concentrated along the line between nuclei.
questions.explanation: A sigma bond is formed by head-on (end-to-end) overlap of atomic orbitals along the internuclear axis. This can involve s-s, s-p, or p-p (end-to-end) overlaps. Pi bonds are formed by sideways overlap.

source_trace.content_anchor: When two atomic orbitals overlap to build up electron density along the axis between the two nuclei, the resulting localized bond is called a sigma bond

explanations_tiered.definition: A sigma bond is a covalent bond formed by end-to-end overlap of orbitals along the bond axis.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Sigma bonds have cylindrical symmetry around the internuclear axis and are the first bond formed between two atoms.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine two hands reaching toward each other and clasping directly. That's like a sigma bond — direct, head-on overlap.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: In a sigma bond, orbitals overlap along the line connecting nuclei. Examples: H₂ (s–s), HCl (s–p end-on), Cl₂ (p–p end-on).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Sigma bonds are characterized by no nodal plane along the bond axis. They are stronger than pi bonds because electron density lies directly between nuclei.
reasoning_steps: Recall the definition of sigma bond: overlap along internuclear axis.
Contrast with pi bond (sideways overlap).
Identify correct option describing end-to-end overlap.
misconceptions: All single bonds are sigma bonds. True, but double/triple bonds also contain one sigma bond.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: σ bond: end-to-end overlap, along bond axis.

glossary_entry.definition: Covalent bond formed by head-on orbital overlap along the internuclear axis.

flashcard.back: End-to-end overlap of atomic orbitals along the internuclear axis.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: End-to-end overlap of orbitals along the internuclear axis.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Covalent bond with head-on orbital overlap.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use diagrams of s-s, s-p, and p-p overlap.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. A sigma bond is end-to-end overlap along the internuclear axis.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: A beryllium chloride (\(\mathrm{BeCl_2}\)) molecule is linear with bond angles of 180°. What hybridization scheme best explains this geometry?
questions.hint: Two electron groups around Be require linear geometry.
questions.explanation: Be in BeCl₂ has two bonding pairs and no lone pairs. Two electron groups arrange linearly (180°). This corresponds to sp hybridization: mixing one s and one p orbital gives two equivalent sp hybrids oriented 180° apart.

source_trace.content_anchor: Be in BeCl2 molecule has an sp hybridization

explanations_tiered.definition: sp hybridization is the mixing of one s and one p orbital to form two equivalent linear hybrid orbitals.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Two electron groups around a central atom give linear geometry (180°) and sp hybridization.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Two 'arms' of orbitals point in opposite directions — straight line. That's sp hybridization.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: BeCl₂: Be has two bonding pairs. To minimise repulsion, they are 180° apart. This requires mixing one 2s and one 2p orbital to form two sp hybrids.
explanations_tiered.advanced: sp hybrids have 50% s character and are directed 180° apart. The linear geometry maximizes distance between electron pairs, minimizing repulsion per VSEPR.
reasoning_steps: Determine the number of electron groups around Be: 2 (both bonds, no lone pairs).
Recall that 2 electron groups → linear geometry (180°).
Linear geometry corresponds to sp hybridization (one s + one p).
misconceptions: sp hybridization only for carbon. False; it occurs for any atom with two electron groups.

glossary_entry.definition: Mixing one s and one p orbital to form two linear hybrid orbitals.

flashcard.front: What hybridization gives a linear geometry?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Be uses sp hybridization, giving two orbitals 180° apart.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that Be is an exception to octet (4 e⁻ total).
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Two electron groups give linear geometry and sp hybridization.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following molecules has a central atom that is \(\mathrm{sp^2}\) hybridized?
questions.hint: Trigonal planar geometry is a signature of sp² hybridization.
questions.explanation: BCl₃ has three bonding pairs and no lone pairs on boron, giving trigonal planar geometry with 120° bond angles. This corresponds to sp² hybridization (one s + two p orbitals). CH₄ is sp³, BeCl₂ is sp, NH₃ is sp³.

source_trace.content_anchor: in BCl3 molecule, the central boron atom... hybridize to form three sp2 hybrid orbitals

explanations_tiered.definition: sp² hybridization is the mixing of one s and two p orbitals to form three equivalent trigonal planar hybrid orbitals.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Three electron groups (with no lone pairs) give trigonal planar geometry and sp² hybridization.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Three balloons tied together form a triangle. That's sp² hybridization — three lobes at 120°.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: BCl₃: B has three bonding pairs, no lone pairs. Electron geometry = trigonal planar, bond angle = 120°. This requires sp² hybrid orbitals.
explanations_tiered.advanced: sp² hybrids have 33% s character and are oriented 120° apart in a plane. The unhybridized p orbital forms π bonds when needed (e.g., in ethene).
reasoning_steps: Identify central atom hybridization from VSEPR.
CH₄: 4 groups → sp³. BeCl₂: 2 groups → sp. BCl₃: 3 groups → sp². NH₃: 4 groups → sp³.
Choose BCl₃.
misconceptions: All trigonal planar molecules are sp² hybridized. True if no lone pairs, but SO₃ is sp² too.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: 3 groups, no lone pairs → sp², trigonal planar.

glossary_entry.definition: Mixing of one s and two p orbitals to form three trigonal planar hybrid orbitals.

flashcard.front: What hybridization does boron use in BCl₃?
flashcard.back: sp² hybridization, giving trigonal planar geometry.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Which molecule has sp² hybridized central atom?
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Point out that BF₃ and SO₃ also sp².
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. BCl₃ has 3 bonding pairs, no lone pairs → trigonal planar → sp² hybridization.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Why is the bond angle in water (104.5°) less than the ideal tetrahedral angle of 109.5°, while methane (CH₄) has exactly 109.5° angles?

options.A: Water has two lone pairs that repel bonding pairs more strongly, compressing the angle.
options.B: Oxygen is more electronegative than carbon, pulling electrons closer.
options.C: Water is bent, not tetrahedral; VSEPR predicts 104.5° for bent shapes.
options.D: Hydrogen bonds in water distort the angle even in a single molecule.

questions.hint: Consider the number and type of electron groups around oxygen vs carbon.
questions.explanation: Both CH₄ and H₂O have tetrahedral electron pair geometry. CH₄ has four bonding pairs, all repelling equally → 109.5°. H₂O has two bonding pairs and two lone pairs. Lone pair–bonding pair repulsion is stronger than bonding pair–bonding pair repulsion, compressing the H–O–H angle to 104.5°.

source_trace.content_anchor: the force of repulsion between a lone pair and a bond pair is more than the force of repulsion between two bond pairs... bond angle is reduced to 104.5°

explanations_tiered.definition: sp³ hybridization involves mixing one s and three p orbitals to form four equivalent tetrahedral hybrid orbitals.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Lone pairs occupy more space than bonding pairs. In water, two lone pairs compress the H–O–H angle. In methane, four identical bonding pairs give perfect tetrahedral angle.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Lone pairs are 'bigger' and push hydrogen atoms closer together, like two people pushing elbows in a crowded elevator, reducing the angle.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Both CH₄ and H₂O are sp³ hybridized. CH₄ has 4 bonding pairs (equal repulsion, 109.5°). H₂O has 2 lone pairs that repel more strongly, squeezing the bonding pairs to 104.5°.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Repulsion strength order: lone pair–lone pair > lone pair–bonding pair > bonding pair–bonding pair. Water has two LP–BP interactions that compress the angle. Methane has only BP–BP interactions.
reasoning_steps: Recall that both molecules have tetrahedral electron geometry (4 electron groups).
CH₄: 4 bonding pairs → symmetric repulsion → 109.5°.
H₂O: 2 bonding pairs + 2 lone pairs → LP–BP repulsion > BP–BP repulsion → bond angle reduced.
misconceptions: Water is not sp³ hybridized because angle is not 109.5°. False; water is sp³ with distorted angle due to lone pairs.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Lone pairs repel more → compress bond angle (H₂O: 104.5°).

glossary_entry.definition: Greater repulsive effect of a nonbonding electron pair compared to bonding pairs.

flashcard.front: Why is H₂O bond angle 104.5° instead of 109.5°?
flashcard.back: Two lone pairs repel bonding pairs more strongly, compressing the angle.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why is the bond angle in water less than in methane?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Lone pairs on oxygen repel bonding pairs more strongly than bonding pairs repel each other.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Lone pairs > bonding pairs in repulsion → smaller angle.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use VSEPR repulsion hierarchy to explain.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain bond angle variations using VSEPR
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Lone pairs exert stronger repulsion, compressing bond angle in water compared to methane.
